_metadata.generated_by: make_agent_knowledge v1.0 (procedural shape; agent-as-drafting-partner fill-in from pre_knowledge/assessments/Designing Stronger Assessments.pptx)
_metadata.description: Trigger-keyed redesign playbooks for strengthening a single assessment via Evidence-Centered Design: clarify core learning, identify convincing evidence, make learning visible, AI-stress-test the visibility, add one architectural improvement.
_metadata.owner: Chaz Clark (BYUI faculty-development; Architects of Learning workshop)
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~9.9k chars / 4 ~= 2,470 tokens — the 2000-8000 'either' band (skill default = embed). Authored override to read_at_runtime: this is one of ~14 sibling knowledge files in canvas-toolbox that an agent loads selectively per task focus (audit vs. redesign), not a stable cache-friendly prefix on every call; and consumers cite back to the source deck and benefit from first-class retrieval citations. Same access pattern as assessments_knowledge.json / backwards_design_knowledge.json.

scope: Actionable sequential procedure for redesigning a single assessment into stronger evidence of learning (Evidence-Centered Design). Boundaries: does not classify/audit assessments (assessments_knowledge.md, reference shape) and does not decide what a course should teach (backwards_design_knowledge.md). The 'do' step that runs after the audit flags weakness; composes with both, replaces neither.

playbooks.name: Full Assessment Redesign (five design decisions)
playbooks.trigger: An instructor wants to strengthen a specific assessment — typically one flagged by the assessments_knowledge.md Litmus Test (low litmus_pass_rate, grading_target: output_only, or assesses_struggle: false) or chosen from a course workbook. One assessment at a time.
prerequisites: Exactly one named assessment selected (not a whole course).
Current intent, task, and rubric available (Canvas-pulled or instructor-supplied via course_ref/).
Instructor present and answering — Decisions 1-2 require their judgment; never infer the core learning from the task title.
steps: Decision 1 — Clarify the Core Learning: what is the most important thing students should learn or be able to do? Push beyond 'what they produce' / 'assignment format' toward capability, reasoning, transfer, habits of mind, disciplinary thinking. Cross-check well-formed outcome (outcomes_quality_knowledge.md) + UbD Stage 1 (backwards_design_knowledge.md).

Decision 2 — Identify Convincing Evidence: what would genuinely convince you the student achieved that learning? Push beyond completion / polished output / correctness toward synthesis, transfer, prioritization, adaptation, judgment, reflection, demonstration, reasoning. (UbD Stage 2.)

Decision 3 — Make the Learning Visible: through what activity/behavior/performance would students reveal that evidence? Forms: oral defense, case analysis, annotation, reflection, draft progression, critique, presentation, simulation, novel-context application, peer review, revision rationale, discussion/debate. Pick the form that surfaces the Decision-2 evidence most directly.

Decision 4 — Stress-Test the Visibility (AI-rich): (a) Could AI complete this without meaningful student engagement? (b) What role SHOULD AI play? (c) What parts reflect authentic student thinking? (d) Are we assessing learning, AI orchestration, or both? Intentional design, NOT prohibition. Reuse assessments_knowledge.md Litmus rows + inverted_blooms_knowledge.md ai_agency classification.

Decision 5 — Strengthen the Architecture: add EXACTLY ONE improvement (overwhelm constraint). Menu: iterative drafts, checkpoints, oral defense, process annotations, AI-use documentation, transfer tasks, peer review, revision reflections. Map the chosen improvement to the specific Decision-4 weakness it closes.
playbooks.success_condition: One redesigned assessment concept with (a) explicit core-learning statement, (b) named convincing evidence, (c) visibility activity, (d) documented AI stress-test result, (e) exactly one applied improvement traceable to a specific weakness. Output as an A3 (toyota_gap_analysis_knowledge.md). Verified by the validity_gate_check playbook.

playbooks.name: AI-Visibility Stress Test (Decision 4 standalone)
playbooks.trigger: Need to judge whether an existing assessment's evidence is trustworthy in an AI-rich environment without a full redesign — e.g., a fast pass during a course audit, or triaging which assessments need full_assessment_redesign first.
prerequisites: AI-agency vocabulary available (inverted_blooms_knowledge.md: ai_dependent / scaffolded / student_owned).
steps: Could AI complete this without meaningful student engagement? (Yes -> high risk.)
What role SHOULD AI play here — and is that role designed in or just unaddressed?
What parts of the artifact reflect authentic student thinking vs. could be AI-produced with no trace?
Is the assessment measuring the learning, the student's AI orchestration, or both — and is that intended?
playbooks.success_condition: A per-assessment trustworthiness verdict (student_owned / scaffolded / ai_dependent, aligned with inverted_blooms_knowledge.md) + one-line reason. Feeds the full_assessment_redesign trigger when verdict is ai_dependent and the core learning warrants it.

playbooks.name: Validity Gate Check (debrief / verification)
playbooks.trigger: A redesign or improvement has been proposed and you must decide whether it actually adds validity. Run before accepting any Decision-5 change and to close the full_assessment_redesign A3.
prerequisites: A concrete proposed change (ideally the single Decision-5 improvement).
steps: Does this redesign improve the evidence of learning (not just deter AI)?
Did it add complexity WITHOUT adding validity? If yes — reject or simplify (overwhelm constraint).

Decisive question: would this change improve learning even if AI didn't exist? If no, it is AI-theater, not stronger evidence — send back to Decision 5. (Slide 12: 'that final question matters most.')
playbooks.success_condition: Proposed change passes step 3 (improves learning independent of AI) AND step 2 (validity gain >= complexity cost). Only then is the full_assessment_redesign success condition satisfied.

sources: BYUI 'Architects of Learning' workshop deck — *Designing Stronger Assessments: Engineering Stronger Evidence of Learning* (13 slides; instructor name to be backfilled). pre_knowledge/assessments/Designing Stronger Assessments.pptx, retrieved 2026-05-18. The deck names its frame 'Evidence-Centered Assessment Design.'

pre_run_checklist: consumed_by[] entries resolve (canvas_course_expert.json verified; ira_program_alignment.md verified)
post_run_checklist: evidence_centered_design_knowledge.md exists with title, scope, body (## Playbooks), provenance, last_updated
shape == procedural and playbooks[] non-empty (3 playbooks)
each playbook has id, name, trigger, prerequisites[], steps[], success_condition
test_cases: Untested in a live course (per Chaz: build first, then test + update). First empirical run: canvas_course_expert flags a weak assessment via assessments_knowledge Litmus, then invokes full_assessment_redesign here.

changes: Initial procedural knowledge file authored (agent-as-drafting-partner) from the BYUI Architects of Learning deck 'Designing Stronger Assessments' (13 slides).
Three trigger-keyed playbooks: full_assessment_redesign (5 design decisions), ai_visibility_stress_test (Decision 4 standalone), validity_gate_check (debrief/verification).

Cross-references: assessments_knowledge.md (diagnostic that triggers this), backwards_design_knowledge.md (Decisions 1-2 ≈ UbD Stages 1-2), inverted_blooms_knowledge.md (ai_agency in Decision 4), outcomes_quality_knowledge.md (Decision 1 outcome quality), toyota_gap_analysis_knowledge.md (A3 output).
Marked v0.1 / untested — to be empirically validated and revised against a real course (Chaz directive: build first, test + update).
